reasoning:
The DFF is tightly controlled by the Fed and has exhibited exactly zero variance from its 4.33% level over the last 6 months (since January 2025). Prior to this pause, the trend was an easing cycle (cuts from 5.33% down to 4.33%). Because day-to-day variance in DFF is almost nonexistent under current Fed operations, an increase would almost certainly require a formal FOMC rate hike. 

1. 2025-06-29 (7 days): extremely unlikely as there is no FOMC meeting scheduled in this window, and quarter-end effects typically don't pull DFF higher mechanically under the current ample reserves framework.
2. 2025-07-22 (1 month): still unlikely given it falls before the late-July FOMC meeting historically.
3. 2025-09-20 (3 months): post mid-September FOMC. Since rates have been paused for 6 months, a hike is a tail risk if inflation proves exceptionally sticky, but continued pause or cuts remains the base case. 
4. 2025-12-19 (6 months): captures multiple late-2025 FOMC meetings; cumulative tail risk of a hike increases over this broader horizon. Uncertainty naturally trends towards 0.5 over time.
